children.name: Pandinus imperator
children.description: Largest scorpion by mass; reaches 20cm, 30g. Native to tropical West Africa. CITES Appendix II. Despite imposing size, venom is mild.

children.name: Pandinus cavimanus
children.description: Found in East Africa; notable for reddish claws.

children.name: Pandinus dictator
children.description: A massive black West African forest scorpion rivalling the emperor scorpion in size; sometimes the largest scorpion species. CITES Appendix II.

children.name: Pandinus gambiensis
children.description: A large West African savanna scorpion of Senegal and the Gambia; like its relatives it has mild venom and relies on powerful pincers.

children.name: Pandinus viatoris
children.description: A large East African scorpion related to the emperor, inhabiting savanna and woodland; like its kin it is heavily collected for the pet trade.

children.name: Heterometrus spinifer
children.description: Large scorpion of Southeast Asian forests; up to 15cm. Relies more on claws than venom. Widely kept as a pet. Glows strongly under UV.

children.name: Heterometrus laoticus
children.description: Among the largest scorpions in Asia; found in Laos, Thailand, Vietnam. Hides in tree bark and leaf litter.

children.name: Heterometrus swammerdami
children.description: Often cited as the world's longest scorpion, reaching about 23 cm; native to India and Sri Lanka. Powerful pincers, mild sting.

children.name: Heterometrus longimanus
children.description: A glossy black rainforest scorpion of Southeast Asia, common in the pet trade; defensive but with low-toxicity venom.

children.name: Heterometrus cyaneus
children.description: A Southeast Asian forest scorpion with a bluish-black sheen, inhabiting humid leaf litter and burrows.

children.name: Heterometrus petersii
children.description: A large forest scorpion of southern China and Indochina with golden-yellow legs, popular as a hardy terrarium species.

children.name: Heterometrus silenus
children.description: A dark, heavy-bodied forest scorpion from the Philippines, occurring in humid lowland forest leaf litter.

children.name: Heterometrus indus
children.description: A glossy black forest scorpion of the Indian subcontinent and Sri Lanka, common in plantations and forest floor habitats.

children.name: Heterometrus laevigatus
children.description: A large, smooth-bodied forest scorpion from Sulawesi and nearby islands, dwelling in humid lowland rainforest.

children.name: Heterometrus mysorensis
children.description: A heavy-bodied forest scorpion from the Mysore region of southern India, sheltering under stones and logs in moist forest.

children.name: Scorpio maurus
children.description: A stout, sand-colored burrowing scorpion of North Africa and the Middle East with very large pincers; digs spiral burrows in arid soil.

children.name: Scorpio palmatus
children.description: A golden, broad-clawed desert scorpion of the Levant and Egypt that excavates deep burrows; mild venom relative to buthids.

children.name: Scorpio fuscus
children.description: A burrowing scorpion of the eastern Mediterranean, recently separated from Scorpio maurus, digging into firm soils on hillsides.

children.name: Opistophthalmus capensis
children.description: A robust southern African burrowing scorpion with forward-set eyes used to monitor its burrow entrance; defensive and quick to stridulate.

children.name: Opistophthalmus glabrifrons
children.description: A large southern African scorpion with contrasting yellow legs and dark body, digging burrows in hard savanna soils.

children.name: Opistophthalmus boehmi
children.description: An East African burrowing scorpion with a strikingly tri-colored body, inhabiting dry savanna and digging beneath rocks.

children.name: Opistophthalmus wahlbergii
children.description: A pale, sturdy burrowing scorpion of the Kalahari, excavating deep spiral burrows in sandy soils to escape heat.

children.name: Opistophthalmus carinatus
children.description: A robust southern African burrowing scorpion with strongly keeled pincers, common in arid savanna soils.

children.name: Opisthacanthus asper
children.description: A flattened, rock-dwelling scorpion of African woodlands that shelters in crevices and under bark rather than digging burrows.

children.name: Opisthacanthus elatus
children.description: A slender crevice-dwelling scorpion; members of this genus have a wide tropical distribution and flattened bodies for hiding under bark and stones.

children.name: Cheloctonus jonesii
children.description: A southern African burrowing scorpion with a long, slender metasoma, inhabiting grasslands and digging shallow burrows.

children.name: Hadogenes troglodytes
children.description: An extremely flattened southern African scorpion that lives in narrow rock cracks; among the longest scorpions and very long-lived, with extremely mild venom.

children.name: Hadogenes paucidens
children.description: A flattened crevice-dwelling scorpion of southern African rocky outcrops, sheltering by day in tight cracks.

children.name: Hadogenes bicolor
children.description: A slim, dorsoventrally compressed scorpion adapted to life within rock fissures of southern Africa, with contrasting body and limb coloration.

children.name: Pandinoides cavimanus
children.description: An East African forest scorpion with a dark body and reddish pincers, once placed in Pandinus; common in the terrarium trade.
